title: Cheap Apartments for Rent in Cleveland
metaTitle: Cheap Apartments for Rent in Cleveland, OH
metaDescription: Cheap apartments for rent in Cleveland from around $700/month. Real price ranges by bedroom, affordable areas, and Section 8-friendly homes. Book a showing.
answerBox: Cheap Cleveland apartments generally start in the $700-$900/month range for a studio or 1-bedroom in an older building, well below Zumper's July 4, 2026 citywide median of $1,250. Demand is high — apartments.com listed roughly 535 units under its "cheap" filter for Cleveland as of that same date. Every home we manage also accepts Section 8 vouchers.

sections.h2: What counts as a "cheap" apartment in Cleveland?
body: In Cleveland, "cheap" generally means renting well below the citywide median. Per Zumper's July 4, 2026 report, the overall median asking rent is $1,250/month, with studios averaging $1,060 and 2-bedrooms at $1,100. Renters searching for budget options are typically looking at units priced from roughly $700 to $950 — older housing stock, smaller square footage, and buildings without on-site amenities.

Demand for this segment is strong: as of July 4, 2026, apartments.com listed roughly 535 units under its "cheap" filter for the Cleveland search area, reflecting how many renters are shopping this price tier at once. Our own portfolio of 90+ rental homes across Greater Cleveland runs from about $700 to $1,800/month, with a typical rent around $1,000 — squarely in the affordable range most "cheap apartment" searches are targeting.

sections.h2: Realistic price ranges by bedroom count
body: Prices vary by neighborhood and condition, but here's what "cheap" tends to look like across our managed homes and the broader Cleveland market. Two-bedroom units make up the largest share of affordable inventory citywide, followed by 3-bedrooms.

rows: Smaller buildings, often older housing stock
Most common size in our managed portfolio
Often a full unit in a duplex or triplex

sections.h2: Which Cleveland areas have the most affordable stock?
body: Affordability tends to track with the age and density of housing stock rather than any single "cheap" district. Neighborhoods with a large supply of early-20th-century duplexes and triplexes — including **Slavic Village (44105)**, **Collinwood (44110)**, **Glenville (44108)**, **Fairfax/Central (44104)**, and **Hough (44103)** — generally have lower average asking rents than downtown or University Circle. We manage rental homes concentrated in these same East and Southeast Cleveland areas, plus a smaller number on the west side around Cudell/Detroit-Shoreway (44102) and Old Brooklyn (44109). Our [neighborhood rental guides](/cleveland-rentals/) break down rent ranges and transit for each area in more detail.

sections.h2: What to watch for when a price seems too good to be true
body: A very low advertised rent can be a legitimate older unit — or a rental scam. Never wire money or pay a deposit before touring a unit and meeting a verifiable property manager, and be wary of listings that pressure you to pay before seeing the home. We don't guarantee any specific unit or rent price sight-unseen; every applicant goes through the same screening process, and we'll walk you through exactly what a home costs and includes before you apply.

If you're working with a Housing Choice Voucher, affordability isn't just about the advertised rent — it also depends on the voucher's payment standard for your household size. Every home we manage accepts Section 8 and is HUD-inspection-ready, which can make budgeting more predictable. See our [Section 8 housing guide](/section-8-housing-cleveland-oh/) for how CMHA vouchers and payment standards work in Cuyahoga County.

sections.h2: How to see a cheap apartment near you
body: The most reliable way to find an affordable home that's actually available is to [book a free showing](/p/book-showing) with our local team and tell us your budget and preferred area. If you've already found a home you like, you can [start an application online](/apply) through our secure tenant portal.

faq.q: What's the cheapest rent I can expect in Cleveland?
faq.a: Some Cleveland units rent for as little as $700/month, typically smaller or older units in multi-family buildings. Zumper's July 4, 2026 report shows a citywide studio median of $1,060, so anything meaningfully below that is on the lower end of the market. Our own portfolio's lowest rents start around $700.

faq.q: Are cheap apartments in Cleveland in high demand?
faq.a: Yes. As of July 4, 2026, apartments.com listed roughly 535 units under its "cheap" filter for the Cleveland area, and affordable units tend to move quickly. Booking a showing as soon as you find a listing that fits your budget improves your odds.

faq.q: Do cheap apartments in Cleveland accept Section 8?
faq.a: It varies by landlord — Ohio doesn't require it statewide. Every home Rent Finder Cleveland manages accepts Housing Choice Vouchers and is HUD-inspection-ready, and our typical rents (around $1,000/month) line up well with many voucher payment standards.

faq.q: Which Cleveland neighborhoods have the most affordable apartments?
faq.a: Neighborhoods with a large stock of older duplexes and triplexes — including Slavic Village, Collinwood, Glenville, Fairfax/Central, and Hough — generally run more affordable than downtown or University Circle. We manage homes concentrated in these same East and Southeast Cleveland areas.

faq.q: Is a cheap apartment automatically a bad deal?
faq.a: No, but verify before you pay anything. Confirm the property manager is legitimate, tour the unit in person when possible, and never send money before seeing the home. A lower rent in an older building is common in Cleveland and isn't itself a red flag.
